did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning (specifically VGG-16) for feature extraction from dermoscopic images and a hierarchical classifier consisting of Random Forest (RF), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Adaboost to diagnose melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel model using CNN with transfer learning (VGG-16) and a hierarchical classifier (RF, KNN, Adaboost) to diagnose melanoma. It includes an experimental setup using the ISIC 2017 dataset, presents original results with performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy), and discusses limitations and future work, indicating it is an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Using Transfer Learning and Hierarchical Classifier to Diagnose Melanoma From Dermoscopic Images
journal: International Journal of Healthcare Information Systems and Informatics
brief_summary: This study proposes a model to diagnose melanoma from dermoscopic images using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning (VGG-16) for feature extraction and a hierarchical classifier comprising Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Adaboost. The experimental evaluation utilized the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge dataset, which was resampled and augmented to address class imbalance. Performance was assessed using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics across different values of K for the KNN component. The results demonstrated that the proposed hierarchical classifier outperformed a baseline classifier combining SVM and KNN. The study concludes that the proposed approach is effective for melanoma detection, highlighting the utility of combining transfer learning with hierarchical classification strategies.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for diagnosis; 374 cases in the training subset of the ISIC 2017 dataset.
Included in the ISIC 2017 training dataset as a non-melanoma class; 254 cases in the training subset.
Included in the ISIC 2017 training dataset as a non-melanoma class; 1,372 cases in the training subset.
Refers to the combined class of benign nevi and seborrheic keratoses used in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a model to detect and classify melanoma from dermoscopic images. The hierarchical classifier (RF, KNN, Adaboost) predicts discrete diagnostic categories: 'melanomic' or 'non-melanomic'. The study uses the ISIC 2017 dataset with classes melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and benign nevi, but explicitly frames the task as classifying images as 'melanomic or non-melanomic' and evaluates performance using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy for melanoma detection.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Classification Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, containing dermoscopic images collected from leading clinical centers internationally. The dataset includes clinical metadata and annotations by skin cancer experts.

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction model (VGG-16) and the hierarchical classifier (RF, KNN, Adaboost). The training subset was resampled and augmented (rotation, flipping) to address class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing (600 images) to evaluate the performance of the proposed hierarchical classifier against a baseline classifier (SVM + KNN).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to construct an ensemble of classifiers (RF, KNN, Adaboost) by collecting votes from component classifiers and assigning the class label with the highest votes.
Used to evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed hierarchical classifier (RF+KNN+Adaboost) against a baseline classifier (SVM+KNN).

Mentioned in the Related Work section as a statistical test performed in a cited study (Brinker et al., 2019b) to compare deep learning network results with dermatologists; not explicitly stated as used in the current study's experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing for its own experimental results. It mentions McNemar's tests in the Related Work section as having been performed in a cited study (Brinker et al., 2019b), but does not apply statistical significance tests to compare its proposed hierarchical classifier against the baseline SVM+KNN classifier.

ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Classification Cha.experiment: Comparison of Proposed Hierarchical Classifier (VGG-16 + RF + KNN + Adaboost) vs. Baseline Classifier (VGG-16 + SVM + KNN)

ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Classification Cha.notes: The paper states that the proposed hierarchical classifier performs better than the baseline classifier consisting of SVM and KNN. It also notes that as the value of K increases, the performance of the classifier decreases. Specific numerical values for sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy are referenced as being in Table 3 and Figure 5, but the exact numbers are not present in the provided text chunks.

architecture_used.main_model: VGG-16 (Convolutional Neural Network with transfer learning) combined with a hierarchical classifier consisting of Random Forest (RF), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Adaboost.
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-16 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (comprising over 1.2 million images and over 1000 classes). The fully connected layers of VGG-16 were removed to use it as a feature extractor.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 × 224 × 3 (RGB images resized to this dimension for VGG-16 input).

feature_extractor.layers: 13 convolution layers and 3 fully connected layers (before removal).
feature_extractor.filters: 3 × 3 convolutional filters with a stride of 1.
feature_extractor.output_features: 512-dimensional feature vector produced by the feature extractor.

levels.role: Verification of non-melanomic cases classified by RF.

classifier.integration_method: Majority Voting ensemble of KNN and Adaboost decisions.

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation and flipping applied to the training subset to address class imbalance.

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Majority voting for the final decision between KNN and Adaboost at the second level.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: 138 million (for the original VGG-16 model; specific parameter count for the hierarchical classifier components is not reported).

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing dermoscopic images to 224 × 224 × 3 to match VGG-16 input requirements
Data augmentation (rotation and flipping) applied to the training subset to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training subset of the ISIC 2017 dataset to address class imbalance. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to the training subset of the ISIC 2017 dataset to address class imbalance. Specific axis or probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed model, which combines VGG-16 for feature extraction with a hierarchical classifier (Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Adaboost), is effective for detecting melanoma from dermoscopic images.
The hierarchical classifier outperforms the baseline classifier consisting of Support Vector Machine (SVM) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN).

The primary advantage of the hierarchical classifier is to improve the sensitivity of the system for correctly detecting melanoma, specifically by verifying non-melanomic cases classified by the Random Forest component.
As the value of K increases in the K-Nearest Neighbor component, the performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the classifier decreases.
A limitation of the study is that features extracted by the CNN were directly used for training without feature selection, which increases complexity and training time.
Another limitation is the use of a binary classifier rather than a multi-class classifier.

Future work will focus on constructing a multi-class classifier for different types of skin cancers and performing feature selection using meta-heuristic techniques to enhance accuracy and reduce complexity.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) rather than a multi-class classification of specific skin cancer types.
No feature selection was performed on the CNN-extracted features, which the authors acknowledge increases classifier complexity and training time.
The training dataset (ISIC 2017) was highly imbalanced (only ~20% melanoma cases), requiring resampling and data augmentation (rotation and flipping) to mitigate bias.
Specific numerical performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) are not reported in the text; results are only referenced as being in Tables 3 and Figure 5.
The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC 2017 dataset for both training and testing.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the significance of the performance difference between the proposed hierarchical classifier and the baseline SVM+KNN classifier.
The K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) component's performance was found to decrease as the value of K increased (tested at K=1, 3, 5).
